Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: OOPA Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: On 2015-07-30, OnOnPay Vietnam Mobile Services JSC assigned its intellectual property, including the domain names "ononpay.com" and "ononpay.vn", to OOPA Pte Ltd under a Deed of Assignment.

On 2017-08-09, Bui Sy Phong and Ms Nguyen Thi Van assigned the shares in their names to OOPA by a Deed of Assignment. OOPA beneficially owned OnOnPay as to 60% directly and 40% indirectly, comprising 38% via Bui and 2% via Van.
OOPA Pte Ltd was set up in Singapore for the purpose of holding the Vietnamese operating company, OnOnPay Vietnam Mobile Services JSC.
Bui secured investors, including Captii Ventures Pte Ltd as lead investor.
In the second half of 2018, a number of options for a potential exit were considered.
At around the same time in late 2018, OnOnPay began to undertake a procurement and supply business for small retailers in Vietnam, described internally as the Central Supply Business.

On 2019-01-11, Captii sent an email to Bui Sy Phong referring to OOPA's ownership of intellectual property used in the Central Supply Business, including the merchant list and the domain name "ononpay.com".

On 2019-01-16, from his OnOnPay email address, Bui Sy Phong sent an email to the directors and shareholders of OOPA summarising updates about OnOnPay, stating that for the recent 1.5 months OnOnPay had launched a central supply platform branded as Telio for mom-and-pop shops in Vietnam, that this new service line had shown good traction in revenue growth and profitability, that it had attracted investment interest, and suggesting the establishment of a new entity whose capitalisation table would fully reflect OnOnPay's current one.

On 2019-01-21, a new Singapore holding company named Telio Pte Ltd was incorporated by Bui Sy Phong as its sole shareholder. A new operating company, wholly owned by Telio, was then incorporated in Vietnam.
On 2019-01-28, Telio Vietnam Co Ltd was incorporated in Vietnam as a wholly owned subsidiary of Telio Pte Ltd.

On 2019-01-25, Ms Nguyen Thi Van emailed OOPA's company secretary, copying Bui Sy Phong, requesting preparation of OOPA board resolutions to approve the spin-off of the merchandising of goods business into Telio and its Vietnamese subsidiary, to approve the structure of the new entity to run the spin-off business, and to approve the transfer of human resources, assets and liabilities from existing OOPA-OnOnPay into Telio SG and Telio VN, as well as new investment into Telio.

On 2019-01-25, an attachment to Van's email set out two options for the new corporate structure, including an option under which the existing shareholders of OOPA would share a percentage of the shareholding of Telio on a basis mirroring their shareholding in OOPA.

On 2019-01-24, Low Zhen Hui from Captii emailed Bui Sy Phong outlining anticipated steps for a new holding structure, including a step where Bui would transfer 100% ownership of Telio to OOPA Group holdco.

On 2019-01-28, Bui Sy Phong forwarded Low Zhen Hui's 2019-01-24 email to OOPA's company secretary, copying Van, and asked the company secretary to be prepared for related actions concerning the steps outlined in Low's email.

On 2019-01-30, Bui Sy Phong emailed Ng Sai Kit attaching a transition plan from OOP to Telio, stating that Telio and OOPA would be held by basically the same group of shareholders, that Telio would reach out to merchants currently on OOPA's payment network, and that all current human resources and other OnOnPay resources would be moved to Telio.

On 2019-01-14, in a Facebook Messenger conversation with a representative of potential investor Nextrans, Bui Sy Phong described the Central Supply Business as having been launched about 1.5 months earlier, serving mom-and-pop shops and F&B outlets with monthly revenue of US$200,000 and high merchant order frequency, and stated that this business would be separated from OnOnPay and placed under Telio, for which he was seeking $200,000 in investment at a valuation of US$1 million.

On 2018-12-15, Bui Sy Phong posted on Facebook that telio.vn had been launched in Hanoi and Ho Chi Minh City to supply merchants with central ordering, fast delivery and transparent pricing, and stated that this confirmed OOP's vision to support the growth of 1.2 million mom-and-pop shops with supply and financing services.
OOPA Pte Ltd owns the whole of OnOnPay beneficially: 60% directly and 40% indirectly; 38% via Bui and 2% via Van.
At the material time, the directors of OOPA were Bui Sy Phong, Ng Sai Kit, Kenneth Tan Wei Chin, Liu Tact Yew and Helmie Anis Ashiblie.
Bui was also a director of OnOnPay. None of the other directors of OOPA were also directors of OnOnPay.
Low Zhen Hui was from Captii and assisted Ng in relation to OOPA.
Neither the top-up business nor the e-wallet business were successful.
Soon after, Bui applied for Telio to enter an accelerator program for start-ups known as Surge Ventures, which was backed by a major venture capital firm, Sequoia Capital.
Bui holds 72.09% of OOPA. It is common ground that upon dilution of Bui's stake in OOPA pursuant to certain agreements, Bui's shareholding in OOPA would be 40.15%.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the Central Supply Business and the Telio corporate structure, developed using OOPA group resources and OnOnPay operations before the dispute, should be treated as a corporate opportunity and asset of OOPA or as an independent venture owned solely by Bui Sy Phong.
Party_Details.Rule: A company director is generally use company resources and business developments for the benefit of the company and its corporate group, and not to appropriate for personal benefit a business opportunity that has been developed or actively pursued using those resources and within the company's operational structure.
Party_Details.Application: Before the dispute, OOPA, through its wholly owned subsidiary OnOnPay, developed the Central Supply Business using OOPA-owned intellectual property, merchant networks and staff. During late 2018 and early 2019, this business was branded as Telio and discussed at OOPA board level, with proposals that Telio's shareholding structure mirror OOPA's capitalisation table and that OOPA's existing shareholders receive a defined percentage of Telio. Corporate diagrams and emails circulated among OOPA directors and its company secretary contemplated OOPA board resolutions to approve the spin-off of the Central Supply Business into Telio and the transfer of OnOnPay resources into Telio. At the same time, Bui Sy Phong incorporated Telio Pte Ltd and Telio Vietnam Co Ltd, became Telio's sole shareholder, and used the performance metrics of the Central Supply Business to approach external investors such as Nextrans and Surge Ventures. These pre-dispute events the Central Supply Business and the Telio structure were developed within the OOPA group framework, while legal title to Telio's shares was initially placed in Bui's name and external funding arrangements were pursued through Telio.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether OOPA was the proper plaintiff, the nature of the Central Supply Business (CSB) as a maturing business opportunity, and whether that opportunity belonged to OOPA. It applied the principle from Canadian Aero Services Ltd v O'Malley, as approved in Tokuhon, that a director is disqualified from usurping for personal benefit a maturing business opportunity which the company is actively pursuing. The judge found that the CSB had moved beyond a mere idea, based on contemporaneous communications where Bui described good traction, revenue growth and profitability, and where he reported to OOPA's board about the CSB and proposed a new entity whose cap table would reflect OOPA's. The court held that the CSB was being undertaken by OnOnPay using OOPA's intellectual property and resources, and that OOPA, as holding company, had the right to decide whether to keep the CSB within OnOnPay or spin it off into Telio. Emails from Van to the company secretary, copied to Bui, requesting OOPA board resolutions to approve the spin-off and transfer of resources, and Bui's own follow-up emails, were treated as evidence that Telio was being incorporated on OOPA's behalf. The judge rejected Bui's contention that the CSB was his personal idea and that only OnOnPay, not OOPA, could complain. The court concluded that OOPA was the proper plaintiff and that the same conduct could constitute breaches of duty to both parent and subsidiary without negating OOPA's rights. On breach, the judge found that Bui moved the CSB into Telio, denied OOPA's ownership of Telio, and caused Telio to enter into arrangements with Surge without informing OOPA, thereby usurping a maturing business opportunity pursued by OOPA. The court held that Bui owed fiduciary duties of loyalty and candour to OOPA and that he failed to discharge them. In assessing an express trust, the court examined the three certainties. It inferred certainty of intention from Bui's 16 January 2019 email proposing a new entity whose cap table would fully reflect OOPA's, and from subsequent correspondence showing that Bui was expected to transfer Telio shares in accordance with a structure to be agreed by OOPA. Certainty of subject matter was found because all Telio shares issued at incorporation were to be held on trust for OOPA until directions were given, and certainty of objects was satisfied because OOPA was identified as the beneficial owner. The judge therefore held that Bui received and held the Telio shares as OOPA's nominee on express trust. In the alternative, the court held that even if an express trust had not been established, an institutional constructive trust would arise over the Telio shares as the fruits of Bui's usurpation of OOPA's corporate opportunity, drawing an analogy with Guy Neale v Nine Squares Pty Ltd, where trade marks registered in a company name were held on trust for a partnership. On the Companies Act s 391(1) defence, the court considered whether Bui had acted honestly and reasonably. It found that Bui knew the CSB did not belong to him personally, relying on his Facebook post describing Telio as confirming OOPA's vision, his use of OnOnPay's CSB track record in investor pitches, and his participation in emails treating Telio as an OOPA group spin-off. The judge rejected Bui's explanations as implausible and held that he had not acted honestly or reasonably, so no relief under s 391(1) was available. The court also declined to grant an equitable allowance, citing Mona Computer Systems v Singaravelu Murugan and emphasising that such allowances should be sparingly granted and that Bui's lack of honesty and candour militated against any allowance. The judge noted that Bui would still benefit indirectly through his shareholding in OOPA and that any loans he made to OnOnPay remained debts. As to remedy, the court declared that all Telio shares registered in Bui's name, whether issued at incorporation or later, were held on trust for OOPA, either under an express trust or, for later-issued shares, under a constructive trust as fruits of the breach. The court ordered Bui to transfer his Telio shares to OOPA and to account for any dividends, with reimbursement to Bui for any incorporation expenses or capital contributions he had made in respect of those shares, but without interest and without allowance for non-financial contributions or personal loans for Telio's development. Costs were awarded to OOPA on a standard basis, including disbursements for its expert, and specific costs orders were made for interlocutory applications. The final outcome was that Bui was held liable to OOPA, and OOPA obtained declaratory and proprietary relief over the Telio shares.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Bui Sy Phong

Facts.Text: By 2018, Bui Sy Phong was a Vietnamese entrepreneur who described himself as a serial entrepreneur and had developed an idea for a start-up venture initially involving top-up tools for Vietnamese mobile subscribers and subsequently an e-wallet application for small retailers in Vietnam.
By 2018, Bui Sy Phong had secured investors for his venture, including Captii Ventures Pte Ltd as lead investor and Gobi MAVCAP ASEAN Investment Management Limited as another investor.
By 2018, Bui Sy Phong was a director of OOPA Pte Ltd and a director of OnOnPay Vietnam Mobile Services JSC.
By 2018, Bui Sy Phong held 72.09% of the shares in OOPA Pte Ltd, with an agreed dilution structure under which his shareholding would become 40.15% upon implementation of certain agreements.

From around 2018-11-01, as founder and CEO of OnOnPay, Bui Sy Phong oversaw the commencement of a procurement and supply business for small retailers in Vietnam, internally referred to as the Central Supply Business.

On 2018-12-15, Bui Sy Phong posted on Facebook that telio.vn had been launched in Hanoi and Ho Chi Minh City to supply merchants with central ordering, fast delivery and transparent pricing, and stated that this confirmed OOPA's vision to support the growth of approximately 1.2 million mom-and-pop shops with supply and financing services.

On 2019-01-14, in a Facebook Messenger conversation with a representative of potential investor Nextrans, Bui Sy Phong described the Central Supply Business as having been launched about 1.5 months earlier, serving mom-and-pop shops and F&B outlets with monthly revenue of about US$200,000 and high merchant order frequency, and indicated that the business would be separated from OnOnPay and placed under a new entity named Telio, for which he was seeking US$200,000 in investment at a US$1 million valuation.

On 2019-01-16, Bui Sy Phong emailed the directors and shareholders of OOPA from his OnOnPay email address, reporting that for the recent 1.5 months OnOnPay had launched Telio as a central supply platform for mom-and-pop shops in Vietnam, stating that the new service line had shown good traction in revenue growth and profitability and had attracted investment interest, and suggesting the establishment of a new entity whose capitalisation table would fully reflect the existing capital structure for the Telio business.
On 2019-01-21, Bui Sy Phong caused the incorporation of Telio Pte Ltd in Singapore, with himself as the sole shareholder, to serve as a new holding company for the Central Supply Business.
On 2019-01-28, Telio Vietnam Co Ltd was incorporated in Vietnam as a wholly owned subsidiary of Telio Pte Ltd to operate the Central Supply Business, with Telio Pte Ltd controlled by Bui Sy Phong.

On 2019-01-24, Bui Sy Phong received an email from Low Zhen Hui of Captii outlining a proposed structure in which Bui would transfer 100% ownership of Telio Pte Ltd to a new OOPA group holding company, and Bui responded by circulating corporate structure diagrams showing options for Telio's ownership.

On 2019-01-25, an email from Ms Nguyen Thi Van to OOPA's company secretary, copied to Bui Sy Phong, requested preparation of OOPA board resolutions to approve the spin-off of the merchandising of goods business into Telio and its Vietnamese subsidiary, the structure of the new entity, and the transfer of human resources, assets and liabilities from OOPA-OnOnPay into Telio Pte Ltd and Telio Vietnam Co Ltd.

On 2019-01-28, Bui Sy Phong forwarded Low Zhen Hui's 2019-01-24 email to OOPA's company secretary, copying Van, and asked the company secretary to be prepared for related corporate actions concerning Telio and the proposed holding structure.

On 2019-01-30, in an email to OOPA director Ng Sai Kit, Bui Sy Phong attached a transition plan from OOPA to Telio and commented that Telio and OOPA would be held by basically the same group of shareholders, that Telio would reach out to merchants currently on OOPA's payment network, and that all current human resources and other OnOnPay resources would be moved to Telio.
By early 2019, Bui Sy Phong was using the performance and merchant base of the Central Supply Business operated through OnOnPay to approach external investors for funding of Telio.
By early March 2019, Bui Sy Phong had applied for Telio Pte Ltd to participate in the Surge Ventures accelerator program for start-ups backed by Sequoia Capital.
On 2019-03-19, acting on behalf of Telio Pte Ltd, Bui Sy Phong signed a term sheet and a convertible note agreement with Surge Ventures for investment into Telio.
On 2019-04-15, at a board meeting of OOPA convened by Ng Sai Kit to discuss the ownership of Telio and the financial situation of OOPA and OnOnPay, Bui Sy Phong participated as a director of OOPA.
By early 2019, it was common ground among the parties that upon dilution of his stake in OOPA pursuant to certain agreements, Bui Sy Phong's shareholding in OOPA would be 40.15%.
By early 2019, Bui Sy Phong was the registered holder of 99,181 ordinary shares in Telio Pte Ltd out of 146,500 issued shares, according to Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority records.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the dispute, the Central Supply Business and Telio-related arrangements developed using OnOnPay and OOPA resources should be treated as belonging to OOPA or as a separate venture owned by Bui Sy Phong, and whether Bui's pre-dispute conduct in incorporating and structuring Telio and seeking external investment created personal exposure for the final outcome.
Party_Details.Rule: A director who develops a new business line or corporate structure using the resources, intellectual property and operational platform of an existing company is generally align the ownership and benefits of that business with the company and its stakeholders, and to avoid structuring or funding arrangements that divert the economic benefit of that business away from the company without transparent approval.
Party_Details.Application: Before the dispute, Bui Sy Phong held a controlling shareholding and board position in OOPA and OnOnPay, and he initiated and oversaw the Central Supply Business within OnOnPay using OOPA-owned intellectual property and merchant networks. He publicly associated the Telio-branded supply platform with OOPA's vision and used the CSB's revenue and merchant metrics in discussions with potential investors. At the same time, he incorporated Telio Pte Ltd and Telio Vietnam Co Ltd with himself as sole shareholder of Telio, while internal emails and diagrams circulated among OOPA directors and the company secretary contemplated that Telio's shareholding would mirror OOPA's capitalisation table and that OOPA board resolutions would approve the spin-off and transfer of resources. Bui forwarded these proposals and asked the company secretary to prepare for related actions, but he also proceeded to sign a term sheet and convertible note agreement with Surge Ventures on behalf of Telio. These pre-dispute steps positioned Telio as the vehicle for the CSB, with legal title to Telio's shares in Bui's name and external funding arrangements being made through Telio, while OOPA's board had not yet implemented a final structure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered Bui's arguments that OOPA was not the proper plaintiff, that any misconduct related only to OnOnPay, that the Central Supply Business (CSB) was his personal idea, and that the CSB had not matured into a business opportunity belonging to OOPA. It rejected these arguments, holding that a director can owe fiduciary duties to both a parent and its subsidiary and that the same conduct can infringe duties to both. The judge found that the CSB was a maturing business opportunity actively pursued within the OOPA group, based on Bui's own contemporaneous communications describing traction, revenue and profitability, and his 16 January 2019 email proposing a new entity whose cap table would fully reflect OOPA's. The court held that OOPA, as holding company, had the right to decide whether to house the CSB within OnOnPay or in a new entity, and that Bui's incorporation of Telio and Telio Vietnam, and his use of OnOnPay's CSB track record to solicit investors, occurred in that context. The judge found that emails from Van to the company secretary, copied to Bui, requesting OOPA board resolutions to approve the spin-off and transfer of resources, and Bui's forwarding of Low's email and corporate diagrams to the company secretary, showed that Telio was being set up on OOPA's behalf. The court did not accept Bui's later claim that he disagreed with Van's email, noting that he raised no contemporaneous objection and that his subsequent emails were consistent with the spin-off plan. The judge concluded that Bui understood that the CSB and Telio structure were to be aligned with OOPA's shareholder base and that he was expected to transfer Telio shares in accordance with a structure to be agreed by OOPA. On the question of honesty and the Companies Act s 391(1) defence, the court found that Bui knew the CSB did not belong to him personally, relying on his Facebook post describing Telio as confirming OOPA's vision, his investor communications referencing OnOnPay's CSB performance, and his participation in internal emails treating Telio as an OOPA group spin-off. The judge rejected Bui's explanation that he misrepresented ownership to investors to justify giving OOPA investors free shares, finding this implausible and inconsistent with the contemporaneous record. The court held that Bui did not act honestly or reasonably when he proceeded to sign investment documents with Surge Ventures on behalf of Telio without informing OOPA or obtaining its consent, in contrast to his earlier practice of keeping OOPA informed about potential investors such as Nextrans. As a result, the court declined to grant relief under s 391(1). On remedies, the judge held that Bui's Telio shares were either held on express trust for OOPA, based on the three certainties, or, in the alternative, on constructive trust as the fruits of his usurpation of OOPA's corporate opportunity. The court ordered Bui to transfer his Telio shares to OOPA and to account for dividends, with limited reimbursement for any incorporation expenses or capital contributions but no equitable allowance for his efforts or loans in developing Telio, given the need to avoid encouraging fiduciaries to act in breach. The court therefore held Bui liable and granted proprietary relief in favour of OOPA.
